pada: नमो॑ । अश्वे᳚भ्यः । अश्व॑-पतिभ्यः । च॒ । वः॒ । नमः॑ ॥
krama: नमो॒ अश्वे᳚भ्यः । अश्वे᳚भ्यो-ऽश्व॑पतिभ्यः । अश्व॑पतिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमः॑ । नम॒ इति॑ नमः॑ ॥

sayana.english:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | ashvebhyah | ashva-patibhyah | iti | ashva-pati-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 17 ||

|| Thus ends the Third Anuvaka. ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo ashvebhyah'—to those with the bodies of horses. Some say: 'ashvah' means those who have no personal property (svam dhanam), the 'a-shvas'; to those who are property-less (ascetics). [Note: some read 's' and 'sh' as interchangeable]. 'Ashvapatibhyashcha vo namah'—to those who are supervisors of horses, or to the very wealthy. All these are either attendants of the God or specific play-forms of the God himself. (17)

Now for the Purashcharana of this anuvaka: having performed Krichhra penance, one achieves siddhi by five thousand chants. When there are epidemics (janapada-vyadhi), the king should have a peace-rite performed. The chanting should be done by eleven perfected Brahmins in a temple of the Lord. Five chant, five perform homa, and one Brahmin offers sesame-rice for each salutation. A lakh (100,000) of chants should be performed. When this is done, the epidemic perishes. In case of diseases of vehicles (horses, elephants, etc.), by offering eleven thousand oblations of cakes mixed with sesame paste, the common diseases of vehicles perish. For other diseases, in the place where vehicles are kept, with Apamarga rice and ghee...
...
